metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` and `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` to the Files section (or a separate 'docs to update' note) and enumerate the specific lines that reference the two-ruleset Terraform path and the `check "deploy_key_present"` block.
findings.finding: The plan removes the `tag_lock` resource and its output from `main.tf`, but `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` (lines 64–65, 68, 177–178) and `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md` (lines 432–433) are unenumerated consumers that will become factually wrong: they describe Terraform Path A as creating 'gerrit-mirror-lock-main + -tags' (two rulesets) and explicitly reference the `check "deploy_key_present"` block by name. Neither file appears in the Files section.
findings.location: Files section; infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md lines 64-65, 68, 177-178; docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md lines 432-433
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Files section, add `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` and `docs/gerrit-aws-setup.md`. In the runbook, update Path A's description to 'creates gerrit-mirror-lock-main (main-only; tag lock intentionally removed)' and replace the mention of `check "deploy_key_present"` with a note about the lifecycle precondition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the live ruleset id (18402431) against `gh api repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="gerrit-mirror-lock-main") | .id'` and record the confirmed id in `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` or `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` comments before the import step.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the lock was applied via gh-api during WS7 (ruleset gerrit-mirror-lock-main, id 18402431)' as a confident fact, but a full repo search finds id 18402431 ONLY in the plan-review cache — never in any source, infra, runbook, or script file. The runbook (github-mirror-lock.md:162) explicitly defers 'reconciling the snapshot/Terraform to the live ids' as a follow-up, meaning this id is unconfirmed in any authoritative repo artifact.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section — 'ruleset gerrit-mirror-lock-main, id 18402431'; AC4 — 'terraform import of the live ruleset (rebar:18402431)'
findings.suggested_fix: Run `gh api repos/navapbc/rebar/rulesets --jq '.[] | select(.name=="gerrit-mirror-lock-main") | .id'`, confirm it returns 18402431, and add a one-line comment in main.tf above `main_lock` noting the live id (e.g., `# live ruleset id: 18402431 (verified <date>)`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering note to the plan (or a runbook step) stating that AC1 (remove tag_lock from main.tf) MUST be completed and the file saved before running `terraform import` / `terraform apply` (AC4), to prevent unintended tag_lock creation.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the apply ordering constraint: the current main.tf still contains github_repository_ruleset.tag_lock (no state entry). Running `terraform apply` before AC1 removes tag_lock would CREATE the tag ruleset on GitHub, immediately breaking release.yml v* tag pushes. The plan lists AC1 (remove tag_lock) and AC4 (import + apply) but does not explicitly order them or warn that an apply on the un-edited file is destructive.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 and AC4 (ordering relationship); infra/terraform-github/main.tf lines 93-117 (tag_lock resource still present)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` to `github_repository_ruleset.main_lock` so accidental `terraform destroy` or replace is rejected, consistent with the pattern used for other critical resources in this repo.
findings.finding: github_repository_ruleset.main_lock has no lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }. An accidental `terraform destroy`, a workspace wipe, or a rename-triggered replace would silently delete the mirror lock and immediately unblock all human pushes to main — defeating the entire security posture of WS7.
findings.location: infra/terraform-github/main.tf — resource "github_repository_ruleset" "main_lock" (line 66)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the test artifact for AC5: add its file path to the Files section and state the test type (e.g., a `tests/infra/test_terraform_github.py` using `grep`/`hcl2`, or a shell script), so the test can be independently run and verified.
findings.finding: AC5 asserts 'a contractual test asserts the config is main-only + has the precondition (no tag_lock)' but a repo-wide search finds zero test files for the terraform-github configuration. The Files section lists only `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`, with no test file path. The test artifact is completely absent from the codebase and unspecified in the plan.
findings.location: AC5 — 'a contractual test asserts the config is main-only + has the precondition (no tag_lock)'; Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the contractual test file path (e.g. `infra/terraform-github/tests/main_lock.tftest.hcl` or equivalent) to the Files section and specify the test framework so the deliverable is fully covered.
findings.finding: The plan describes a 'contractual test' deliverable (AC5: 'a contractual test asserts the config is main-only + has the precondition') but the Files section lists only `infra/terraform-github/main.tf` — no test file is named, leaving the test deliverable without a covering file path.
findings.location: AC5 vs Files section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Files: `- infra/terraform-github/tests/main_lock.tftest.hcl` and note that it uses `terraform test` (or conftest) to assert: (a) no `github_repository_ruleset` resource named tag_lock exists, and (b) a `lifecycle.precondition` block exists on main_lock.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the contractual test file path (e.g. `infra/terraform-github/tests/main_only_test.go` or equivalent) to the Files section so its location and framework are explicit.
findings.finding: AC5 requires a 'contractual test' file (asserting main-only config and precondition presence), but the plan's Files section lists only `infra/terraform-github/main.tf`. A new test file is a CREATION (new behavior) that is out-of-scope for the declared edit surface.
findings.location: AC5; Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Specify whether `terraform import` is CLI-invocation or a declarative `import {}` block in `main.tf`; if declarative, add it to the Files list so the import address `rebar/18402431` (or `rebar:18402431`) is code-reviewable.
findings.finding: AC4 specifies 'terraform import of the live ruleset (rebar:18402431) into main_lock' but does not state whether this will use the CLI form (`terraform import`) or a declarative `import {}` block (Terraform >=1.5). The Files section lists only `main.tf`, implying no new file — yet a declarative import block either belongs in `main.tf` (making it reviewable as code) or is done purely via CLI (leaving no file artifact and no code review trail). The plan's AC4 is the only step that reconciles live state with TF state and its mechanism is unspecified, leaving the import unauditable if CLI-only.
findings.location: AC4: 'Live reconcile: terraform import of the live ruleset (rebar:18402431) into main_lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a declarative import block to `main.tf` (Terraform >= 1.5 supports it, and `required_version >= 1.10` satisfies this): `import { to = github_repository_ruleset.main_lock; id = "rebar:18402431" }`. Remove it after the first successful apply, or retain it as a no-op with a comment. Update the Files section to note this addition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC2: Confirm the `lifecycle { precondition }` condition expression explicitly replicates the key-title lookup from the removed `check` block (i.e., `length([for k in data.github_repository_deploy_keys.all.keys : k if k.title == var.deploy_key_title]) > 0`), and confirm the `data.github_repository_deploy_keys.all` data source is RETAINED to feed it.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 specifies replacing the `check` block with a `lifecycle { precondition }` on `main_lock` but does not state what condition expression the precondition will use. If the `data.github_repository_deploy_keys.all` data source is dropped or the condition is weakened (e.g., simplified away from the `for` comprehension that checks `k.title == var.deploy_key_title`), the fail-closed guarantee is illusory: applying the mirror-lock with a missing deploy key would lock everyone out of `main`, including Gerrit replication.
findings.location: AC2 — 'The check "deploy_key_present" block is replaced by a lifecycle { precondition } on the main_lock resource so a MISSING replication deploy key ABORTS apply (fail-closed)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Files/AC2 description, add: 'The `data.github_repository_deploy_keys.all` data source is preserved; the precondition condition is `length([for k in data.github_repository_deploy_keys.all.keys : k if k.title == var.deploy_key_title]) > 0` — identical logic to the removed `check` block, but now abort-on-failure rather than warn-only.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC5) that exercises the fail-closed behavior: e.g., a test that mocks/simulates the deploy-key data source returning zero keys and asserts `terraform plan` or `terraform apply` exits non-zero with the precondition error message.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the fail-closed precondition behavior end-to-end (i.e., verifies that a `terraform apply` actually aborts when the deploy key is absent); AC5's contractual test only asserts structural presence of the precondition block, not that it fires correctly.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A test (e.g., using Terratest or a tfmock provider override) runs terraform plan with the deploy-key data source returning an empty set and asserts exit code ≠ 0 and stderr contains the precondition error_message string.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or add) a scheduled CI job (e.g., `terraform plan` on a cron) or GitHub Actions drift-detection workflow so deploy-key deletion and other drift are surfaced within an acceptable SLA, not just at the next manual apply.
findings.finding: The plan adds a lifecycle precondition that gates all future `terraform apply` runs on deploy-key presence, but provides no observability for the deploy-key state BETWEEN applies (e.g., if the key is deleted after the last apply, operators have no alert until the next apply attempt fails). This leaves a silent failure window of unknown duration.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (precondition block)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a GitHub Actions workflow on schedule (e.g., daily) that runs `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` and fails/alerts if exit code 2 (changes detected) or 1 (error, which would include a failing precondition). This closes the silent-failure window between applies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or test step) that exercises the precondition's **failure path**: run `terraform plan` against a configuration where the deploy key data source returns no results (e.g., using a mock provider or a test workspace with no deploy key), and assert that the plan/apply is aborted with the expected precondition error — not merely that the precondition block is present in the file.
findings.finding: The only test for the critical new `lifecycle { precondition }` (fail-closed deploy key gate) is a structural/grep assertion that the precondition block 'exists in the file.' This is a source-grep masquerading as a behavioral test: it confirms the text is present but never exercises whether the precondition actually aborts `terraform apply` when the deploy key data source returns no results. If the condition expression is wrong (wrong data source attribute, inverted boolean, etc.), the grep passes and the gate silently fails open in production.
findings.location: AC bullet 5: 'a contractual test asserts the config is main-only + has the precondition (no tag_lock)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test step (e.g., using `terraform test` with an `override_data` block to simulate a missing deploy key, or a separate Terratest/shell test) that runs `terraform plan` with the deploy key absent and asserts the exit code is non-zero with a message matching the precondition error string. This gives a behavioral oracle for the fail-closed guarantee rather than a structural grep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle the plan (or add an explicit deferral note) to reflect that 'drift detection' here means a one-time reconcile + apply-time precondition guard, not a recurring drift-check; if an ongoing drift-detection job is intended, add it as a deferred AC or a separate ticket reference.
findings.finding: The plan title says 'drift detection' which implies an ongoing or recurring detection mechanism, but the plan actually delivers a one-time state reconcile plus a precondition guard (fail-closed on missing deploy key). No periodic drift-detection capability (e.g., a CI drift-check job, scheduled plan, or Atlantis policy) is introduced or deferred. The title over-promises relative to what is built.
findings.location: Title / What section
findings.suggested_fix: Change title to 'Terraform-github state reconcile + mirror-lock precondition hardening' and add a note such as 'Ongoing drift detection (scheduled plan runs / CI drift-check) is out of scope for this ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact Terraform expression or data source the `lifecycle { precondition }` condition uses to detect deploy-key absence (e.g., `data.github_repository_deploy_keys.mirror` filtered for the replication key fingerprint/title) so the executor has no design choice to make.
findings.finding: The precondition's condition expression — what Terraform data source or input it reads to determine deploy-key presence — is not specified, deferring a real design choice (which resource/data source to evaluate) to the executor.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'condition = length([for k in data.github_repository_deploy_keys.mirror.keys : k if k.title == "gerrit-replication"]) > 0 — sourced from a data.github_repository_deploy_keys data block targeting the repo; error_message = "Replication deploy key is absent — apply aborted."'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path for the import step: document `terraform state pull > pre-import-backup.tfstate` before import and `terraform state rm github_repository_ruleset.main_lock` as the back-out, and require a `terraform plan` review before any `terraform apply` post-import.
findings.finding: The `terraform import` step (AC4) is a state-mutation operation with no rollback or back-out plan: if the import captures wrong state or the wrong resource ID, a subsequent `terraform apply` could plan a destructive diff against live GitHub resources.
findings.location: AC4: 'Live reconcile: terraform import of the live ruleset (rebar:18402431) into main_lock'
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend to AC4: 'Back up state (`terraform state pull > pre-import-backup.tfstate`), then `terraform import github_repository_ruleset.main_lock rebar:18402431`, verify `terraform plan` shows No changes before any apply; rollback via `terraform state rm github_repository_ruleset.main_lock` if plan is unexpected.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md/DECISIONS.md entry capturing (a) why tag_lock was removed (main-only scope, release.yml v* push compatibility) and (b) why the deploy-key check is fail-closed (security invariant), so future contributors have durable context.
findings.finding: The main-only architectural decision (removing tag_lock) and the new fail-closed precondition pattern are novel decisions for this repo, but the plan only captures them in inline Terraform comments (AC3). No ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update is called for, so the rationale is invisible to future contributors who won't read the tf file's comments.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (stale comments reconciled) and AC1 (tag_lock removal)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `infra/terraform-github/DECISIONS.md` (or append to repo-root CLAUDE.md) with two entries: one for 'main-only mirror-lock scope (WS7 decision, tag pushes must remain human-pushable)' and one for 'deploy-key precondition is intentionally fail-closed to prevent silent replication breakage'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback/recovery procedure for the fail-closed precondition: e.g., 'if deploy key is absent and apply must proceed, temporarily comment out the precondition, apply, then restore it after the key is re-provisioned.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a fail-closed behavior change (lifecycle precondition that ABORTs apply when deploy key is absent) with no documented rollback or recovery procedure for operators who encounter a broken apply.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2 (precondition replacing deploy_key_present check)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback / Recovery' section to the plan (or inline comment in main.tf) that states: (1) if the precondition fires unexpectedly, restore the deploy key first; (2) if an emergency apply must proceed without the key, the precondition block can be temporarily removed and re-added post-recovery.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit decision gate after `terraform import`: if `terraform plan` shows any changes other than 'No changes', the plan must specify whether to (a) update the Terraform config to match live state, (b) apply to reconcile live state to config, or (c) abort and escalate — with a documented approval step before any apply that modifies the live ruleset.
findings.finding: The plan specifies running `terraform import` then `terraform plan` and expecting 'No changes', but provides no guidance on what to do if plan shows unexpected drift — a one-shot apply of unreviewed changes could silently modify the live ruleset.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #4 (Live reconcile: terraform import … then terraform plan reports 'No changes')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/github/rollback-mirror-lock.sh` as a consumer in the plan and decide whether to update it (e.g. make tag-ruleset deletion conditional / add a comment that TF is now main-only) or explicitly document that the script remains the authoritative remover of the tag lock independent of TF.
findings.finding: `infra/github/rollback-mirror-lock.sh` hardcodes `TAG_RULESET_NAME="gerrit-mirror-lock-tags"` and unconditionally attempts to delete it. After this plan removes `tag_lock` from Terraform state, the script becomes inconsistent: if the live tag ruleset also doesn't exist, the rollback silently skips it; if someone applied tag_lock manually, the script's scope diverges from TF. This consumer is not enumerated in the plan.
findings.location: Files section; infra/github/rollback-mirror-lock.sh line 27

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in AC2 (or the What section) stating that Terraform `check` block assertions are non-blocking warnings in TF ≥ 1.5 while `lifecycle { precondition }` hard-aborts apply — and that the `check` block is removed (not kept alongside), with the runbook reference at `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md:68` updated accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for WHY replacing `check "deploy_key_present"` with a `lifecycle { precondition }` achieves fail-closed semantics — specifically, that Terraform `check` block assertions (>=1.5) produce non-blocking warnings and do NOT abort `apply`, while `lifecycle { precondition }` hard-aborts apply before the resource is created/modified. Without this rationale, an implementer has no documented basis for the design choice and may inadvertently keep the `check` block alongside the precondition (making both fire redundantly) or revert the change believing both are equivalent.
findings.location: AC2 — 'The check "deploy_key_present" block is replaced by a lifecycle { precondition }'
findings.suggested_fix: Augment AC2 to read: '…replaced by a `lifecycle { precondition }` on the `main_lock` resource (and the `check` block removed) — rationale: `check` assertions in Terraform ≥ 1.5 are non-blocking warnings that do not halt apply; `lifecycle { precondition }` hard-aborts apply before the resource is touched, making the gate truly fail-closed. Update the runbook reference at infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md:68 to reflect the new mechanism.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
